question: What recent randomized controlled trials evaluate GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy for type 2 diabetes?

42259343.population: Adults aged 18+ with type 2 diabetes, HbA1c 7.0-10.5%, BMI 23+
42259343.intervention: Oral elecoglipron (small molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist), multiple doses
42259343.comparator: Placebo and open-label oral semaglutide 14 mg once daily
42259343.main_findings: Elecoglipron reduced HbA1c by -0.91% to -1.88% versus -0.15% with placebo at 26 weeks, with a safety/tolerability profile consistent with the GLP-1 receptor agonist class.
42259343.statistical_significance: Mean HbA1c change -1.88% (95% CI -2.23 to -1.53) for the highest elecoglipron dose

42173109.population: Adults 18-74 years with type 2 diabetes for 6+ months, HbA1c 6.5-10.5%, BMI 25-50
42173109.intervention: Subcutaneous GIP infusion alone or added to semaglutide
42173109.comparator: Placebo plus placebo, or semaglutide plus placebo
42173109.main_findings: 6 weeks of GIP infusion did not improve glycaemic control as add-on to either placebo or semaglutide.
42173109.statistical_significance: p=0.13 (placebo+GIP vs placebo+placebo); p=1.00 (semaglutide+GIP vs semaglutide+placebo)

42319968.population: Adults with obesity, type 1/type 2 diabetes, or polycystic ovary syndrome receiving GLP-1RA or SGLT2i
42319968.intervention: GLP-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RA) or SGLT2 inhibitors (SGLT2i)
42319968.main_findings: Both GLP-1RA (MD -1.51 kg) and SGLT2i (MD -1.04 kg) were associated with loss of lean body mass, congruent with overall weight loss.

stub_query_understanding.search_query: semaglutide AND type 2 diabetes AND randomized controlled trial

42259343.population: Adults aged 18+ with type 2 diabetes, HbA1c 7.0-10.5%, BMI 23+
42259343.intervention: Oral elecoglipron (small molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist), multiple doses
42259343.comparator: Placebo and open-label oral semaglutide 14 mg once daily
42259343.main_findings: Elecoglipron reduced HbA1c by -0.91% to -1.88% versus -0.15% with placebo at 26 weeks, with a safety/tolerability profile consistent with the GLP-1 receptor agonist class.
42259343.statistical_significance: Mean HbA1c change -1.88% (95% CI -2.23 to -1.53) for the highest elecoglipron dose

42173109.population: Adults 18-74 years with type 2 diabetes for 6+ months, HbA1c 6.5-10.5%, BMI 25-50
42173109.intervention: Subcutaneous GIP infusion alone or added to semaglutide
42173109.comparator: Placebo plus placebo, or semaglutide plus placebo
42173109.main_findings: GIP infusion as add-on to placebo or semaglutide had an uncertain effect on glycaemic control given the small sample.

42319968.population: Adults with obesity, type 1/type 2 diabetes, or polycystic ovary syndrome receiving GLP-1RA or SGLT2i
42319968.intervention: GLP-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RA) or SGLT2 inhibitors (SGLT2i)
42319968.main_findings: Both GLP-1RA (MD -1.51 kg) and SGLT2i (MD -1.04 kg) were associated with loss of lean body mass, congruent with overall weight loss.

stub_narrative.evidence_summary: Oral elecoglipron, a small-molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist, reduced HbA1c by up to 1.88% versus placebo over 26 weeks in 404 participants with type 2 diabetes [PMID: 42259343]. In a smaller trial of 61 participants, adding subcutaneous GIP infusion to semaglutide did not further improve glycaemic control [PMID: 42173109]. A systematic review and meta-analysis of 36 studies found both GLP-1RA and SGLT2i therapy associated with loss of lean body mass alongside overall weight loss [PMID: 42319968].
stub_narrative.clinical_implications: Oral small-molecule GLP-1 receptor agonists may offer an alternative to injectable therapies; GIP co-administration does not appear to add glycaemic benefit on top of semaglutide.
stub_narrative.limitations: The GIP trial was underpowered (n=61) and single-centre; elecoglipron's phase 2b results require confirmation in phase 3 trials.
stub_narrative.future_directions: Phase 3 trials of elecoglipron and larger GIP/GLP-1 co-agonism studies are needed.
